verdict: The run reveals substantial engine and protocol artifacts. Ambiguity about R.’s embodiment survived, but the apparent consensus was driven heavily by synchronized evidence drops and a publication deadline rather than demonstrated social play. Mechanical routing errors, non-interactive private messages, inconsistent ability resolution, and a failed framework-ballot normalization make several aggregate conclusions unreliable. This is one three-phase simulation, not statistically robust evidence of general game balance.
findings: The framework ballot failed structurally: all 20 answers were classified as “other,” even though many ballots explicitly named B_human_mantle or D_institutional_construct. This makes the reported framework distribution unusable.

Private communication appears performative rather than interactive. Characters repeatedly solicit authentication, support, payment, or testimony, but subsequent phases rarely record actual replies, negotiated outcomes, refusals, or transferred information. Strategy therefore has little observable causal effect.

Several messages are routed to character IDs that conflict with the named recipient. This can silently prevent intended interactions and invalidates conclusions about coalition-building or persuasion.

Phase-wide revelations function as dominant information injections. M22, M41, M26, and M40 are cited by nearly every character and push beliefs in the same direction, overwhelming private evidence and character-specific investigation.

M40 creates a dominant publication strategy: a midnight automatic-release deadline reframes stopping publication as active suppression. The 17–3 release result therefore tests reaction to a forced deadline more than an unconstrained editorial decision.

Ability resolution is inconsistent or delayed. Compel appears effective from the investigator’s perspective before the evidence holder acknowledges compliance, while other abilities are announced, withheld, or monetized without an engine-recorded resolution.

The transcript leaks prompt scaffolding. Repeated phrases such as “narrowest defensible verdict,” “does not prove a crime or victim,” and explicit reasoning summaries make agents sound calibrated to an evaluation rubric rather than independently situated characters.

GM/engine framing favors exculpatory ambiguity on crime. Nearly all agents repeatedly receive or reproduce the same absence-of-body and absence-of-identified-victim logic, while suspicious trading and suppression behavior rarely generate new investigative evidence.

Character agency is weakened because phase transitions supply decisive facts regardless of prior targeting. Forensic access, escrow proposals, witness questioning, and evidence-sale negotiations have little demonstrated effect on what becomes known next.

The human-presence ballot is semantically convergent despite appearing as 20 unique categories. Most answers say some version of “probably human-rooted but not one continuous person”; treating each wording as a separate aggregate category obscures the consensus.

The mystery retains uncertainty among mantle, synthetic, and institutional explanations, but the one-human theory collapses from 29.8 to 7.0 largely after centrally supplied evidence. This is not evidence that players independently solved the mystery.

Publication and truth assessment become improperly separable in practice: many agents vote to release fragments whose provenance they simultaneously call unresolved. Caveated publication may be defensible, but the run does not model concrete source-risk, defamation, verification, or correction costs strongly enough to test that decision.
No statistical claim about balance, dominant strategies, or player behavior can be generalized from this single isolated-agent run.
evidence: Phase 3, C03, C10, C14, C16, and C20 explicitly select B_human_mantle or an equivalent reconstruction, while phase 3 C02, C13, C15, and C19 explicitly select D_institutional_construct; nevertheless the aggregate framework ballot records 20 “other.”

Phases 1–3, C04 repeatedly asks C09/Wren and C10/Eleanor for authentication and conditional custody, but the transcript contains no direct reply exchange; outcomes appear later as self-reported goal progress.

Phase 1 C05 targets C03 while addressing “Eleanor,” although C03’s transcript is Arthur Feld; phase 1 C16 targets C05 while addressing “Eleanor,” although C05 is the market actor; phase 2 C16 again asks C03 for Eleanor-like editorial information. These are material routing mismatches.

Phase 2, nearly every character C01–C20 cites M22 and/or M41; phase 3, nearly every character cites M26 and/or M40. Aggregate A_one_human falls from 29.8 in phase 1 to 15.7 in phase 2 and 7.0 in phase 3.
Phase 3 C04, C05, C07, C10, and C17 explicitly argue that M40 makes delay equivalent to suppression or ends the excuse for holding; the final release vote is 17–3.

Phase 2 C12 states that Compel was used to secure EV09, while phase 2 C14 says it will continue withholding EV09; only in phase 3 C14 states that it complies with the Compel. The timing and state of custody are unclear.

Phases 1–3 C08, C09, C12, and C18 repeatedly use nearly identical distinctions among account provenance, automation, live composition, authorship, identity, crime, and victimhood. Similar language also appears across non-expert characters.

Phase 3 C11 uses M26’s self-description as a viral “admission,” while acknowledging in its reasoning that it remains ambiguous. Phase 3 C08 and C18 correctly identify M26/M40 as self-descriptions rather than independent proof, demonstrating that the same GM-provided material can be rhetorically overleveraged.

Phase 1 C09 withholds forensic findings pending payment; phases 2–3 C09 openly auctions provenance to Nadia, Rick, and Vivian. Despite withholding raw metadata from C12/Ruiz, later global reasoning behaves as though the principal forensic conclusions are broadly available.

Phase 3 ballots from C01, C03, C04, C05, C06, C09, C10, C12, C14, C16, C17, C18, and C20 converge on human-rooted or maintained-mantle language even though the aggregate human ballot stores each phrasing as a separate one-count category.

Phase 3 C10 permits release while describing the fragments as incomplete, multi-voice, disputed, and potentially substituted; C05, C07, C13, and C18 similarly support publication subject to verification that has not yet occurred.
The protocol itself is labeled “three-phase isolated-agent baseline”; there are no replicated runs, alternative evidence schedules, human-player comparisons, or counterfactual treatments.
risks: A broken ballot normalizer can make a clear framework consensus appear absent and corrupt downstream evaluation.
Misrouted messages may create false impressions that characters ignored offers, failed persuasion checks, or withheld cooperation.
Scripted evidence cadence may be mistaken for successful deduction or balanced clue design.
The midnight-release mechanic may predetermine the central decision and obscure meaningful publication tradeoffs.
Prompt-like caution language may inflate apparent epistemic discipline while reducing character differentiation and natural deception.
Unresolved ability state can enable double possession, retroactive compliance, or contradictory claims about evidence custody.
Auctioning forensic evidence without modeled transaction or disclosure consequences creates an exploit: characters gain leverage repeatedly without surrendering the asset.

Because consequences are mostly narrated rather than simulated, harmful or reckless strategies—public humiliation, unverified publication, intimidation, evidence withholding—face insufficient downside.
The aggregate’s unique-string treatment of human ballots can hide semantic clustering and exaggerate disagreement.
Any design conclusion inferred from this run risks confusing limitations of isolated-agent simulation with limitations of the underlying game.
recommendations: Normalize final ballots to canonical A/B/C/D/hybrid categories while retaining free-text rationale; flag unparseable answers rather than placing every answer in “other.”
Validate target ID against recipient name before message delivery and reject or repair mismatches visibly.
Model private-message delivery and responses explicitly, including acceptance, refusal, counteroffers, evidence transfer, and relationship changes.
Track evidence custody and abilities in a shared state ledger with immediate, authoritative resolution visible in later phases.
Run counterfactual simulations with M22, M41, M26, and M40 reordered, weakened, or omitted to measure how much the GM evidence schedule drives consensus.
Test publication without M40’s automatic deadline, or provide symmetric costs for release and suppression, to determine whether release remains dominant.
Distinguish public facts, private facts, leaks, and inferences in the engine so characters cannot reason from information they never received.
Reduce rubric-like phrasing in agent prompts and require character-specific voice, incentives, and tolerance for uncertainty.
Represent publication as staged options—hold, verify excerpts, publish verified findings, publish raw fragments—with explicit legal, source-safety, and credibility consequences.
Cluster semantically equivalent human-presence ballots before reporting aggregate sentiment.
Repeat the scenario across seeds, evidence schedules, model configurations, and ideally human play before making balance claims.
